title: Wake at fixed time
description: Set and maintain a consistent wake time daily (±30 min). The body clock requires regularity to properly time cortisol, melatonin, and testosterone pulses. Even on weekends.

title: Morning sunlight within 30–60 min of waking
description: Get outside within 30–60 min of waking. Look at the sky (not directly at sun) for 30 sec × 2–3 sets. Clear day: ~5 min. Cloud cover: ~10 min. Dense overcast: ~20–30 min. Do NOT get sunlight through glass. No sunglasses (corrective lenses OK). Face and upper body exposed.

title: Phone down until after morning sunlight
description: Delay phone use until after getting outside for morning sunlight and initial movement. Blue light from phone screens before circadian anchoring disrupts cortisol curve and dopamine pathway.

title: Morning supplements with food
description: Take morning supplement set with first meal: Magnesium (partial dose), B-Complex, D3+K2, Zinc 15–30mg, Selenium 100–200mcg, Vitamin C (partial — split across meals), Creatine (partial of 10–15g/day total), Boron 3–6mg. Boron must be morning ONLY — taking it later causes premature waking.

title: Hit protein target: ≥1.6–2.2 g/kg BW
description: Consume ≥1.6–2.2 g/kg bodyweight in protein across all meals. Prioritize animal sources: red meat, eggs, shellfish, clean dairy. Author recommends ~2 g/kg as target. Activate mTOR 3–5 times daily with leucine-threshold doses (30–40g quality protein per meal).

title: Cook only in animal fats — zero seed oils
description: Use only tallow, butter, ghee, or coconut oil for cooking. Eliminate all seed/vegetable oils (sunflower, canola, soybean, corn, safflower, peanut, rapeseed). PUFAs in seed oils directly inhibit 5-alpha-reductase and drive thyroid suppression, oxidative damage, and inflammation.

title: Avoid seed/vegetable oils entirely
description: Zero seed/vegetable oils in any form: sunflower, canola, soybean, corn, safflower, peanut, rapeseed, rice bran. Also avoid foods fried in these oils (restaurants, packaged foods). These PUFAs inhibit 5AR, suppress thyroid, and create oxidative damage at the Leydig cell level.

title: Avoid sugar and liquid fructose
description: Eliminate all liquid sugar, HFCS, sodas, fruit juices, sports drinks with sugar, candy, pastries. Fructose is 10× more reactive than glucose in forming AGEs, goes to liver not muscle, drives aromatase upregulation, and causes a ~25% testosterone drop in human studies.

title: Avoid all soy products
description: Eliminate soy milk, tofu, edamame, soy protein powder, soy lecithin (in processed foods), and soybean oil. Soy isoflavones and phytoestrogens suppress LH and testosterone, upregulate estrogen receptors, and inhibit steroidogenic enzymes.

title: Eat liver 2–3×/week
description: Consume 50–100g of organ meats (beef/chicken liver preferred) 2–3 times per week. Provides vitamins A, B12, folate, copper, choline — the densest micronutrient source in the base diet. Cook gently in butter or tallow; do not overcook.

title: Oysters or shellfish every other day
description: Eat 1 can or 6 large oysters every other day. Best natural source of zinc, copper, selenium, and iodine — all critical cofactors for steroidogenesis, thyroid conversion, and 5AR function.

title: Soak meat in baking soda before cooking
description: Before cooking: soak meat in water + baking soda (5g per bowl of water) for 1–3 hours. This raises pH, softens the meat, and pulls out oxidized fats and stress-related compounds from the muscle. Rinse before cooking.

title: Cook meat in oven in own fat, rare to medium-rare
description: Cook meat in oven using its own fat. Never overcook — rare to medium-rare only. High-heat frying in poor-quality oil or overcooked meat increases oxidized fat load and AGE formation. Avoid grilling at extreme temperatures.

title: Prepare oats with soak + ferment (not plain oats)
description: Oats are PREP REQUIRED — not approved plain. Protocol: soak oats in water, discard the water, then ferment ~24h in raw A2 dairy (kefir or yogurt). This degrades phytic acid (zinc/magnesium blocker), reduces avenin (gut irritant), ferments the starch (flatter insulin spike), and improves mineral absorption.

title: Post-workout: hot starch + lean protein within 30 min
description: Within 30 min of last set: eat 100–200g hot starch (freshly cooked white rice or freshly peeled hot potatoes) + 30–40g lean protein. Fat ≤5–7g. No fructose. GLUT4 stays elevated 2–3h post-training — this window fills glycogen in exact trained fibers.

title: Post-workout first meal: fat ≤5–7g only
description: Keep fat strictly ≤5–7g in the first post-workout meal. High fat in this window slows gastric emptying, creates Randle competition (fat vs glucose for oxidation), and routes carbs toward fat storage instead of muscle glycogen. Fat returns 2–3h later.

title: No fructose in post-workout window
description: Zero fructose in the post-workout nutrition window. Fructose enters via GLUT5 → liver only; it cannot enter muscle cells via GLUT4. Using honey, fruit, or sucrose as intra/post-workout carbs sends fuel to the liver instead of the working muscle fibers.

title: 2–3h post-workout: protein + fat only, no carbs
description: Second post-workout meal (2–3h after first): animal protein + animal fat only (beef, eggs, tallow/butter). No carbs — GLUT4 tail has faded and adding carbs now risks Randle competition and adipose routing. This meal activates mTOR a second time via protein alone.

title: Pre-workout: protein only — zero carbs
description: Pre-workout meal (0–60 min before): 30–40g lean protein (whey isolate, egg whites, lean meat/fish). Fat ≤5–10g. Zero carbs. Low fiber. Pre-carbs spike insulin, shut lipolysis, and create energy mismatch. Protein-only keeps fat burning active while providing amino acid protection.

title: Start intra-workout glucose AFTER 1–2 hard sets
description: Start sipping dextrose or cyclic dextrin drink (30–50g in water + 2g sodium) only after completing the first 1–2 real heavy sets (~15 min into session). Not before. Not at warm-up. Contractions open GLUT4 independently of insulin — glucose then floods directly into working fibers.

title: Warm shower + slow breathing before post-workout meal
description: After the last training set: take a warm shower (~10 min) with slow, deep breathing and extended exhales. This activates the parasympathetic nervous system, shifts blood flow to the gut, and descends cortisol — improving post-workout nutrient absorption and anabolic signaling.

title: Post-workout supplement stack with first meal
description: With the first post-workout meal take: R-ALA 300mg (pure R-form) + HMB 1g (Ca-HMB or BetaTOR) + Taurine 2g + joint stack (MSM 2g + Glucosamine 2g + Chondroitin 1g). R-ALA extends GLUT4 window; HMB activates mTOR + AMPK simultaneously; Taurine supports cell volumization and T4→T3 conversion.

title: Walk 10–15 min after main meals
description: Take a slow 10–15 min walk after main meals (especially the largest meal of the day and post-workout meal). Keeps GLUT4 partially active, moves glucose into muscle instead of fat, lowers insulin faster, and improves digestion.

title: No caffeine within 10–12 hours of bedtime
description: Stop all caffeine at least 10–12 hours before target sleep time. Caffeine has a half-life of ~5–7 hours — even afternoon coffee significantly impairs sleep architecture (especially deep sleep and REM). No caffeine after midday if sleeping at midnight; no caffeine after 10am if sleeping at 10pm.

title: Blue light blockers 1–2 hours before bed
description: Put on blue light blocking glasses 1–2 hours before target sleep time. Lower all screen brightness to minimum. Stop social media scrolling during wind-down window. Blue light suppresses melatonin onset and delays sleep.

title: No bright lights after 10pm
description: Avoid all bright light sources after 10pm: overhead lights, phone screens, TV without screen dimmer. Bright light between 10pm–4am suppresses dopamine and disrupts melatonin — even brief exposure significantly delays sleep onset and shifts the circadian clock.

title: Sleep room temperature: 15–20°C (60–68°F)
description: Verify room temperature is 15–20°C (60–68°F) before sleep. Core body temperature must drop to initiate deep sleep. Higher temperatures prevent entry into deep N3 sleep stages, where testosterone and GH pulses occur. Use a hygrometer/thermometer to verify.

title: Sleep environment: blackout + earplugs + no phone
description: Before sleep: close blackout curtains fully, insert earplugs (Loop Quiet or similar), place phone in another room or face-down on airplane mode. Any light or sound interrupts sleep architecture and reduces deep sleep and REM time.

title: Pre-sleep supplement stack (30 min before bed)
description: Take core sleep stack ~30 min before attempting sleep: Magnesium Citrate/Glycinate 300–500mg + L-Theanine 200–400mg + Apigenin 50mg. Start with these three only. If sleep quality is still poor after 1–2 weeks, add one more at a time: Valerian Root 500mg, Passion Flower 200–400mg, or Lemon Balm 200–400mg.

title: Last large meal 2–3 hours before bed
description: Finish the last large meal at least 2–3 hours before sleep. Eating close to sleep elevates core body temperature, delays sleep onset, fragments deep sleep, and disrupts overnight GH secretion. Protein + fat final meal (no carbs) is optimal for this window.

title: CBD oil: take 5 hours before bed (not at bedtime)
description: If using CBD oil: take 7 drops of 2% CBD 5 hours before bed. CBD has a stimulating effect that reverses 3–4 hours after ingestion into a calming phase. Taking it immediately before bed can cause paradoxical alertness and delay sleep onset.

title: Take Boron in the morning ONLY
description: Boron (3–6mg) must be taken with morning food only. If taken in the evening or afternoon, boron causes premature waking in the early hours of the morning. This is clearly noted in the KB as a timing-critical rule.

title: Rest days: reduce carbs ~25%
description: On rest days reduce carbs by approximately 25% of daily intake vs training day. Borrow those carbs to add to training days (carb cycling). Same weekly total — better body composition results. GLUT4 is mostly closed on rest days so carbs land less efficiently.

title: Drink filtered water; avoid unfiltered tap water
description: Use filtered water for daily drinking. Tap water contains chlorine, chloramine, heavy metals (lead, mercury, arsenic), pesticides, pharmaceuticals, and synthetic hormones from birth control. Minimum: activated carbon filter. Preferred: reverse osmosis or multi-stage system.

title: Blood work prep: 10–12h fast before morning draw
description: Fast for 10–12 hours before blood draw for accurate glucose, insulin, and lipid panel results. No food, coffee, or supplements during the fast. Morning draw is best. Do not draw within 5–7 days of heavy lifting (ALT/AST will be falsely elevated). Do not draw if currently ill or within 1–2 days of very hard training (hsCRP will be falsely elevated).

title: Microneedling: once every 7–14 days
description: Use derma stamp or derma pen (NOT roller) with 0.5–1.0mm needles. Stamp affected scalp areas 3–5 passes per section until mild erythema. Apply treatment oil or serum IMMEDIATELY after — absorption is 10–100× higher post-needling. Do not touch scalp for 6–12 hours to prevent infection.

title: Hair oiling: 2–3×/week
description: Apply carrier oil blend (40% castor + 30% coconut + 20% olive/avocado + 10% jojoba + 10–15% rosemary essential oil) to scalp. Massage 5–10 min. Leave minimum 2h; overnight preferred. Wash with egg or clay. Apply on same day as microneedling while channels are open for maximum absorption.

title: Egg hair washing: 1–2×/week
description: Replace conventional shampoo with raw egg washing 1–2×/week. Crack 2–4 pasture-raised eggs. Apply to wet hair. Massage into scalp 2–3 min. Leave 10–15 min. Rinse with COOL water only (hot water cooks egg and creates residue). Optional: add 1 tsp raw honey. Expect 2–4 week transition period of greasiness.

title: Red light therapy: 3–4×/week
description: Apply red light therapy device to scalp 3–4 times per week, 10–15 min per session. Use 660nm (red) + 850nm (near-infrared) combination at 6–12 inch distance from scalp. Supports follicle mitochondrial function and local circulation.

title: Weekly supplement review
description: Once per week: check what supplements were taken, note observed effects (sleep quality, energy, libido, mood, skin, GI). Confirm no supplement is causing disruption. Note any new addition and intended purpose. Confirm boron is morning-only. Staged tracking is essential for attribution.

title: Monthly protocol and progress review
description: Once per month: review supplement stack (working / drop / add one change at a time), review sleep log averages, review which protocols were consistently applied, note physical changes (body composition, energy, libido, mood, hair density/shedding, skin texture).

title: Hit 8,000+ steps daily
description: Aim for 8,000–12,000 steps/day. Men who walked fewer than 4,000 steps/day had the lowest testosterone; those at 8,000–12,000 had the highest. Start incrementally if not there yet. Walking is the lowest-cost testosterone-supporting activity available.

title: Daily stress management practice
description: At least one daily stress reduction action: walk outside 30–60 min (nature/parks preferred), double breath technique when acutely stressed (inhale 90% → pause → inhale to 110% → full exhale), or end-of-day relaxing activity (reading, social, low-stimulation media). Chronic cortisol steals cholesterol from testosterone synthesis and directly suppresses GnRH.

title: Limit alcohol: ≤2 drinks/week; ideally zero
description: Limit alcohol to ≤2 drinks per week; ideally zero. Alcohol upregulates aromatase, converting testosterone to estrogen. Bedtime alcohol at 0.8 g/kg reduces overnight GH secretion by 70–75%. Moderate intake (~40g/day × 3 weeks) drops total testosterone ~6.8%. GGT rises with even 'moderate' regular intake.
